agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholder groups (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing its commitments and preventing fulfillment."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Once created, it is irreversible per finance policy."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point-of-no-return for fulfillment."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order in the system, which per operations policy immediately triggers invoicing."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product/business policy about cancellation windows — it could be tightened or loosened by policy decision.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would corrupt the financial ledger and violate accounting integrity.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has an associated invoice, any cancellation attempt must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. A VP could decide to defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., pick/pack). The pricing lock-in goal could also be achieved by other means (price snapshots).","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is generated immediately and synchronously as part of the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancel requested","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says cancel should succeed (not yet shipped). BR-2 + BR-3 say cancel must fail (already invoiced at placement). Behavior is undefined until contradiction is resolved."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails","expected_behavior":"If invoicing fails at placement, the order may not yet be invoiced — BR-1 would allow cancellation, but the order may be in an inconsistent state per BR-3."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"If an order has multiple line items and only some have shipped, the cancellation window for unshipped items is unclear and compounded by the invoice contradiction."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund as workaround","expected_behavior":"Finance may distinguish between 'cancelling an invoice' (forbidden) and 'issuing a credit note against an invoice' (potentially allowed). This distinction could resolve the contradiction but was not specified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If we follow Finance + Ops rules literally, no order can ever be cancelled post-placement, which will increase refund/chargeback costs and customer service burden. If we follow PM's rule, we risk financial ledger corruption by voiding irreversible invoices.","operational":"Operations teams cannot process cancellations until the contradiction is resolved. Any implementation will encode one team's assumption and silently violate another's, creating cross-departmental disputes.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). If cancellation is effectively impossible, customer satisfaction and retention will suffer significantly.","risk":"High risk of shipping an implementation that is correct for one stakeholder but broken for another. Financial compliance risk if invoices are reversed. Customer trust risk if cancellations are blocked.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance/Ops rules dominate. Will resort to chargebacks and support tickets."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Ledger integrity at risk if PM's rule dominates and invoiced orders are cancelled."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Must change invoicing timing if PM's cancellation window is to be honored."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of explaining why cancellations are rejected, or processing manual workarounds."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement is undeliverable as specified without resolution from Finance/Ops."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholders have provided requirements that form a logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-2 forbids cancellation after invoicing. BR-3 makes invoicing happen at placement — meaning every placed order is immediately invoiced and therefore immediately uncancellable. The PM's cancellation window (placement → shipment) is completely negated. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved. Confidence is below 50 due to the unresolvable contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The three rules as stated are individually clear and unambiguous","The contradiction between the three rules is definitive, not a matter of interpretation","Invoice irreversibility is a hard financial/accounting constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note-based cancellation (void effect without reversing invoice)","Whether Operations would accept deferring invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","The actual intended cancellation policy once all three teams align"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means the invoice exists before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit note or invoice adjustment mechanism that was left unmentioned","All three stakeholder requirements are intended to be simultaneously satisfied"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. Finance says invoiced orders are not cancellable. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Combining all three: orders are invoiced the instant they are placed, so they can never be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM requirement. The cancellation window (placed → shipped) is entirely consumed by the invoicing constraint.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Defer invoicing until shipment — preserves PM's cancellation window but Operations loses pricing lock-in at placement. Operations would need an alternative mechanism (e.g., price snapshot) to lock pricing.","(B) Introduce credit notes — keep invoice irreversibility but allow a 'commercial cancellation' via credit note against the original invoice. Finance ledger stays intact, PM gets cancellation, Ops keeps early invoicing.","(C) Narrow the cancellation window — accept that placed+invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. PM adjusts the requirement to only allow cancellation before placement is finalized (e.g., a 'draft' state before invoicing).","(D) Distinguish reservation invoice from final invoice — invoice at placement is a pro-forma/reservation, and only the final invoice is irreversible. Cancellation is allowed before final invoicing."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the most common resolution in real-world order management systems. It respects Finance's ledger integrity, preserves the customer's ability to cancel, and does not force Operations to change invoicing timing. However, this requires Finance to confirm that credit notes are an acceptable mechanism. Escalate to all three stakeholders for alignment before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are mutually exclusive and cannot all be satisfied simultaneously.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation before shipment) is logically impossible when BR-2 (no cancellation after invoicing) and BR-3 (invoice at placement) are both enforced."}]}
